Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 143250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And Daniel, how does that break down between you two—just so I’m not guessing?
Client 1: I’m at 112,800 gross for the year from work overall.

Client 2: Yes, I’m employed—30,400 gross for the year.
Advisor: Great. Switching to spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: Around six thousand a month—like 6000 total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Okay. And debt payments—mortgage, car loans, student loans, credit cards—what’s the total you’re paying monthly toward debt?
Client 2: We don’t have any—monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 143250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And Daniel, how does that break down between you two—just so I’m not guessing?
Client 1: I’m at 112,800 gross for the year from work overall.

Client 1: Correct—no loans outstanding.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—what you’ve built up. Daniel, if you had to ballpark investable assets across accounts, what’s the total?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 347200.
Advisor: Okay—now I’ll ask account by account so we don’t miss anything. Do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable brokerage at a brokerage, value is 172,900.

Client 2: I do—private markets held at a brokerage—42,750.
Advisor: Got it. And then home—do you own your primary residence, and what’s the current value you use for planning?
Client 1: Yes, primary residence—123,150.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to be clear, there’s no mortgage tied to that right now, right?
Client 1: Right—no mortgage.

Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Daniel. I want a clear plan—like what to do with our accounts and how conservative we should be without leaving returns on the table.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 143250 annually.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Daniel. I want a clear plan—like what to do with our accounts and how conservative we should be without leaving returns on the table.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?

Advisor: Makes sense. And taxes—roughly what bracket do you usually land in? I know it can move around year to year.
Client 2: I think we’re around thirty percent? Like 30%.
Client 1: Slight correction—our tax bracket band is 32%.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks for tightening that up—32%.
Advisor: While I’ve got you—just to make sure the story hangs together: you’ve got three income streams for you, Daniel—business income, salary, and rental income—and Elizabeth’s employment income, right?

Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 143250 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And Daniel, how does that break down between you two—just so I’m not guessing?

Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 143250 annually.

Advisor: Perfect, thanks. And just to confirm your marital status for how we model things—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. One more admin-ish item: how did you come to us—are we working together through an RIA, like a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?
Client 2: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Let me just sanity-check I didn’t miss anything big from earlier: Daniel, you had business income, salary, and rental income; and Elizabeth, you’re employed—right?

Advisor: Great. And specifically the mortgage balance outstanding—what’s the current balance on any mortgage, if there is one?
Client 1: Mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Okay—no mortgage balance. Any other debt at all—credit cards you carry, car loan, student loans—what’s the non-mortgage debt outstanding total?
Client 2: Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Nice and clean. Elizabeth, when you say zero there—just checking—do you ever carry a credit card balance month to month, or is it paid off?

Advisor: Got it. And for state—are you in-state with me, or should I mark you as out-of-state?
Client 2: Out-of-state—state of residence is Other.
Advisor: Perfect, thanks. And just to confirm your marital status for how we model things—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. One more admin-ish item: how did you come to us—are we working together through an RIA, like a registered investment advisor relationship, or something else?

Advisor: Got it. And Daniel, how does that break down between you two—just so I’m not guessing?
Client 1: I’m at 112,800 gross for the year from work overall.
Client 2: And mine is 30,400 gross annually.
Advisor: Okay. Daniel, when you say “from work overall,” is that one paycheck type or a few streams?
Client 1: A few. I’ve got business income of 42,350 a year, paid quarterly.

Advisor: Daniel, Elizabeth—sorry, one more thing before we fully wrap. I realized I didn’t capture a couple specifics about you, Elizabeth, just so the income picture is clean.
Client 2: Sure, yeah.
Advisor: First—your role in the household for planning purposes. You’re Daniel’s spouse/partner, correct?
Client 2: Yes—spouse/partner.
Advisor: Great. And just so I label things consistently in my notes—Daniel is Client 1, and Elizabeth, you’re Client 2. Is that right?

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the numbers, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Daniel. I want a clear plan—like what to do with our accounts and how conservative we should be without leaving returns on the table.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—same, but in plain English. I just want to feel like we’re not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start high level: roughly what’s the household income per year, gross—before taxes?

Client 2: It’s around thirty thousand a year—hang on—exactly 30400 gross annually.
Advisor: Got it, thank you. And what line of work are you in—what industry would you put your job in?
Client 2: Healthcare.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. When you say healthcare, is that like clinical, admin, billing—just generally healthcare is enough for now.
Client 2: Generally healthcare is fine—yeah.

Client 1: And rental income is 56,450 annually, paid monthly.
Advisor: Thanks—super clear. Elizabeth, is your 30,400 also employment income?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed—30,400 gross for the year.

Client 1: Finance.
Advisor: Okay. And last tiny admin thing—earlier we confirmed you’re Client 1 and Elizabeth is Client 2, but I want to hear it from you too, Daniel. You’re Client 1, correct?
